Welcome to jazz.logs📂! Here I’ll be archiving the finest sounds I come across—from rare grooves to timeless classics.
Just a space to share the music I love❤️.

WEEK 1 | DAY 1

[LOG 001] Monty Alexander - Spunky (1965)

Fresh from Kingston to California. At just 21 years old, Monty released this gem, marking his arrival on the big stage. 🇯🇲✈️🇺🇸
While many pianists take decades to find their voice, Monty had it from the start. This album documents the beginning of a legend, showcasing a terrifying technical ability mixed with pure Caribbean soul.

📝Log Note:

While the whole album is solid, my personal favorite is “Rattlesnake”. The energy on this track is contagious. It’s the perfect evidence of how the three instruments complement each other—the interplay is tight, dynamic, and the groove never stops driving forward.
A masterclass in chemistry. 🔥

💿 Album: Spunky 🎹 Artist: Monty Alexander 📅 Year: 1965 🎼 Genre: Hard Bop / Soul Jazz 🏷️ Label: Pacific Jazz
🎹 Monty Alexander: Piano 🎸 Victor Gaskin / Bob Cranshaw: Bass 🥁 Paul Humphrey / Bruno Carr: Drums 🎸 Gene Bertoncini / Scott Turner: Guitar

I hope you enjoy it as much as I do. I’ll definitely be recommending more Monty in the future. Have a great week!❤️

#jazzlogs #jazz #montyalexander #jazzpiano #vinylcommunity
bestMoment: viernes a la noche para arrancar el fin de semana con energía

editorialNote: Primer disco de jazzlogs, aca empezo un viaje que no tiene fin y es el mejor inicio posible con un album que me llega al alma y siempre me saca una sonrisa,Un debut magnético que captura la esencia del hard bop cruzado con un groove innegable. La energía es constante y contagiosa, destacándose por un interplay dinámico donde el pulso nunca decae, demostrando madurez técnica y una identidad sonora ya definida desde el primer día.
recommendedIf: Si querés entrar al hard bop y al soul jazz con un disco directo, vibrante y lleno de groove que te engancha desde el primer track.
avoidIf: Si buscás un álbum introspectivo, oscuro o de baja energía para escuchar en calma.
albumContext: Debut temprano de Monty Alexander en Pacific Jazz, grabado cuando todavía era muy joven y ya mostraba una identidad clara: hard bop con pulso caribeño, swing directo y una energía pianística muy física. Dentro de Jazzlogs funciona como punto de partida del archivo y como presentación de una sensibilidad alegre, técnica y profundamente groovera.

caption:
Welcome to Day 2🔥! Here is the perfect way to set the mood and unwind—in the hands of Lee Morgan. 🎺🗽

WEEK 1 | DAY 2

[LOG 002] Lee Morgan - Cornbread (1965)

Time to add some serious attitude to the archive. If yesterday was about sunny vibes, today we dive into the cool, smoky energy of the New York scene. 🎺

Lee Morgan was already a heavyweight before this record. By the late 50s, he had recorded Blue Train with John Coltrane and defined the sound of Art Blakey’s Jazz Messengers on Moanin’. After shaking the world with his massive 1963 hit The Sidewinder, Morgan went on a tear during the mid-60s, releasing a string of high-quality albums that defined the Soul-Jazz era. Cornbread lands right in that golden run—proving he was one of the most consistent voices in jazz history.

📝Log Note:
This album is a perfect journey. It only has 5 tracks, and each one transmits a completely different vibe—from the soul-jazz strut of the title track to the moody blues of “Ill Wind”. Yet, they all keep that signature Lee Morgan essence: confident, soulful, and sharp. My personal favorite has to be “Ceora”. It’s a Bossa Nova track that became an instant jazz standard. The melody is incredibly lyrical and elegant; it shows a softer, more melodic side of Lee that balances out the “bad boy” energy of the rest of the record. Also, I have to mention Herbie Hancock on the piano—he simply does everything right. His touch adds so much class to the whole session. Honestly, the whole album is a masterpiece. There are no skips here. 🍊🌑

💿 Album: Cornbread 🎺 Artist: Lee Morgan 📅 Year: 1965 🎼 Genre: Hard Bop / Soul Jazz 🏷️ Label: Blue Note 🎺 Lee Morgan: Trumpet 🎹 Herbie Hancock: Piano 🎷 Hank Mobley: Tenor Sax 🎷 Jackie McLean:Alto Sax 🎸 Larry Ridley: Bass 🥁 Billy Higgins: Drums

A true essential for any collection. Hope this adds some groove to your day! Happy Tuesday!❤️

#jazzlogs #leemorgan #bluenote #jazzvinyl #hardbop
notes: Standout track: Ceora. Soul-jazz energy with a softer melodic side.
whyItMatters: Documenta el pico creativo de Lee Morgan en los 60s y define el sonido definitivo del Soul-Jazz de Blue Note. Es un disco crucial porque equilibra a la perfección la actitud cruda del hard bop con composiciones de extrema sofisticación melódica.
editorialNote: Un álbum sin puntos débiles que encapsula la energía urbana y humeante del jazz neoyorquino de mediados de los sesenta. Con un quinteto estelar y el toque distintivo de Herbie Hancock en el piano, ofrece un viaje sumamente equilibrado entre el groove innegable, la actitud desafiante y momentos de pura elegancia lírica.
recommendedIf: Si buscás sumergirte en el sonido clásico de Blue Note con un disco que destila actitud urbana, groove contagioso y elegancia nocturna en partes iguales.
avoidIf: Si preferís jazz suave, discreto o sin tanta actitud urbana y presencia de trompeta.
albumContext: Grabado en plena etapa dorada de Lee Morgan para Blue Note, Cornbread pertenece a la tradición hard bop y soul jazz de mediados de los sesenta. Llega después del impacto de The Sidewinder y muestra a Morgan combinando actitud urbana, melodías memorables y una banda de altísimo nivel, con Herbie Hancock como figura clave en el color armónico.

caption:
Welcome to VocalWednesdays!🎙️

From now on, the middle of the week belongs to the voices that shaped the soul of jazz.
We are shifting the focus from the instruments to the storytellers.
And to kick things off, there was simply no other choice: we had to start with The Queen herself.👑

WEEK 1 | DAY 3

[LOG 003] Aretha Franklin - Aretha (with The Ray Bryant Combo) (1961)

Before she demanded “Respect” at Atlantic and became a global icon, an 18-year-old Aretha Franklin walked into Columbia Recording Studios in NYC to take her very first steps in the music industry. This isn’t just a record; it’s a historical document of a young prodigy finding her voice. Paired with the Ray Bryant Combo, this album captures the raw, unfiltered moment the world was first introduced to her talent.

📝Log Note: From start to finish, this album displays a unique energy and raw talent that literally keeps you glued to the speakers until the needle hits the run-out groove. It is packed with standards, but Aretha attacks them with such freshness that they feel brand new. My personal favorite has to be “Right Now”. The swing on that track is infectious; it captures her youthful confidence perfectly, backed by the tight, driving rhythms of the Ray Bryant Combo. It’s impossible to listen to it without tapping your foot. It wasn’t just a debut; it was a warning shot to the industry. Simply put, the Queen created magic. 🎹👑

💿 Album: Aretha (with The Ray Bryant Combo) 🎤 Artist: Aretha Franklin 📅 Year: 1961 🎼 Genre: Vocal Jazz / Pop 🏷️ Label: Columbia
A perfect start to our vocal journey. Hope this touches your soul today! Happy Wednesday! ❤️

#jazz #jazzlogs #arethafranklin #vinylcommunity #vocaljazz
notes: Standout track: Right Now. Start of VocalWednesdays.
whyItMatters: Es un documento histórico invaluable que captura a una Aretha Franklin de apenas 18 años dando sus primeros pasos. Demuestra cómo, incluso cantando estándares de jazz y pop antes de su explosión en el soul, ya poseía una voz, un swing y una confianza magnética que cambiarían la industria.
editorialNote: Un debut vibrante y lleno de frescura. Lejos de la megaestrella en la que se convertiría años después, aquí encontramos a una joven prodigio interpretando clásicos con una energía cruda y un swing contagioso. El acompañamiento de Ray Bryant proporciona el marco perfecto y dinámico para una voz que exige atención desde el primer fraseo.
recommendedIf: Si querés una puerta de entrada elegante al jazz vocal y te intriga descubrir el talento puro y crudo de una de las mejores voces de la historia antes de su salto al estrellato masivo.
avoidIf: Si buscás jazz instrumental puro o una escucha más sobria, sin protagonismo vocal tan marcado.
albumContext: Primer álbum de Aretha Franklin para Columbia, registrado antes de su explosión soul en Atlantic. El disco captura a una cantante de apenas 18 años enfrentándose a standards de jazz y pop con una autoridad vocal inusual, acompañada por el Ray Bryant Combo. Es importante porque documenta el nacimiento de una voz histórica antes de convertirse en icono global.

WEEK 1 | DAY 4

[LOG 004] Ike Quebec - Blue & Sentimental (1961)

Ike Quebec wasn’t just a saxophonist; he was the unsung hero behind Blue Note Records, the talent scout who actually discovered legends like Thelonious Monk and Bud Powell.
But after disappearing from the scene in the 50s due to severe personal struggles and health issues, he returned in the early 60s for a final run of recordings. This album is the crown jewel of that comeback.
Recorded just over a year before his tragic death, it captures a man pouring a lifetime of pain and experience into his instrument.
It is widely considered his absolute masterpiece—not because he plays the fastest notes, but because he plays with the most soul.

📝 Log Note: This album is special to me for one big reason: the chemistry between Ike and the guitarist, Grant Green(who is a massive favorite of mine). My absolute standout track is “Blues for Charlie”. In my opinion, this is where you can appreciate the dynamic between Green and Ike to the absolute max. The interplay is effortless, raw, and full of space.
If you dig the guitar work here, keep your eyes peeled for the next logs... I might be dropping some pure Grant Green heat next week. 🎸👀

💿 Album: Blue & Sentimental 🎤 Artist: Ike Quebec 📅 Year: 1961 🎼 Genre: Jazz 🏷️ Label: Blue Note Records
[Session: Dec 16, 1961] (Main Album) 🎷 Ike Quebec - Tenor Sax & Piano 🎸 Grant Green - Guitar 🎻 Paul Chambers - Bass 🥁 Philly Joe Jones - Drums
[Session: Dec 23, 1961] (Track: “Count Every Star”) 🎹 Sonny Clark - Piano 🎻 Sam Jones - Bass 🥁 Louis Hayes - Drums

#jazz #jazzlogs #ikequebec #bluenote #vinylcommunity

Demuestra que la madurez emocional y el manejo de los silencios valen tanto como la destreza técnica, destacando además por una formación inusual (sin piano en casi todo el disco) que le da todo el espacio al diálogo crudo entre el saxo y la guitarra.

La interacción descarnada y llena de aire con la guitarra de Grant Green genera un blues lento, honesto y maravillosamente melancólico, priorizando el sentimiento por sobre la velocidad.

albumContext: Blue & Sentimental pertenece al regreso final de Ike Quebec a Blue Note a comienzos de los sesenta. Es un álbum marcado por el tono maduro, nocturno y blusero del saxo tenor, con una formación especialmente espaciosa donde la guitarra de Grant Green ocupa un rol central. Su valor está en la emoción contenida, el silencio y la vulnerabilidad antes que en la velocidad.

WEEK 1 | DAY 5

[LOG 005] Art Blakey & The Jazz Messengers - Moanin’

Art Blakey wasn’t just a drummer; he was the headmaster of the “University of Hard Bop.” For decades, The Jazz Messengers served as the ultimate proving ground for young talent.
If you survived Blakey’s intense rhythm, you were ready for anything. In 1958, he assembled this specific lineup to make a statement.
They wanted to reclaim Jazz from the “Cool” style and bring back the fire, the blues, and the church roots. This album isn’t just music; it’s the definition of the genre.

📝 Log Note:

As we saw in Cornbread earlier this week, Lee Morgan is unmistakable here. Everyone talks about the title track (and for good reason), but for me, the real highlight is “Along Came Betty”.
The way Morgan’s trumpet floats over the piano chords is pure magic. It’s lyrical, elegant, and drives the whole track perfectly.

💿 Album: Moanin’ 🥁 Artist: Art Blakey & The Jazz Messengers 📅 Year: 1958 🎼 Genre: Hard Bop / Jazz 🏷️ Label: Blue Note
🎺 Trumpet: Lee Morgan 🎷 Tenor Sax: Benny Golson 🎹 Piano: Bobby Timmons 🎻 Bass: Jymie Merritt 🥁 Drums: Art Blakey

WEEK 1 | SUNDAY

[LOG 006] Ryo Fukui - Scenery (1976)

Ryo Fukui is a unique case in jazz history.
Unlike most prodigies who start as children, he taught himself to play the piano at age 22.
For decades, this album remained a treasure in Japan, mostly ignored by the Western mainstream.
But in recent years, it has resurfaced to become a global cult classic and the undisputed “gateway” into the world of Japanese Jazz (J-Jazz).
It blends the hard-swinging style of American bop with a distinct Japanese sensitivity—melancholic, precise, and incredibly crisp.
It captures the sound of a late-night Tokyo jazz bar perfectly.

📝 Log Note:

This album is special to me because it proves you don’t need a lifetime of theory to play with soul.
My absolute standout track is “Willow Weep For Me”. What I love most about it is how the rhythm changes throughout the song. It starts slow and moody, but it keeps building up tension, carrying you along until it finally releases at the very end. The way the trio handles that buildup is just masterful. 🎹🌙

💿 Album: Scenery 🎹 Artist: Ryo Fukui 📅 Year: 1976 🎼 Genre: J-Jazz / Post-Bop 🏷️ Label: Nadja / Trio Records [Session: 1976] 🎹 Ryo Fukui - Piano 🎻 Satoshi Denpo - Bass 🥁 Yoshinori Fukui - Drums

caption:
Welcome to Week 2! 👋 🎷We are kicking things off with absolute greatness: the one and only Sonny Rollins and his undeniable magic.
There is no better way to start the week than with the “Saxophone Colossus” himself.

WEEK 2 | DAY 1

[LOG 007] Sonny Rollins - Sonny Rollins and the Contemporary Leaders (1958)

This isn’t just a session; it’s a summit meeting. In 1958, Sonny Rollins (the Titan of East Coast Hard Bop) traveled west to Los Angeles to record for Contemporary Records.
The label surrounded him with the absolute best “West Coast” players of the time. The result? A unique blend of East Coast fire and West Coast cool.

Historically, this album is heavy: it was the last recording Sonny made before his legendary 3-year retirement from the music scene.
He went out on a high note.

📝 Log Note:

The texture here is unique because of the guitar/piano combination.
My standout track is “How High the Moon”. It’s a bit more relaxed than the rest of the album, and that’s exactly why I love it.
This is where you can truly appreciate the quality of Sonny’s tone—it is big, warm, and incredibly articulate. You can hear every nuance of his saxophone. It’s a total masterclass in phrasing and control. 🎷✨

Track 3) 🥁 Shelly Manne - Drums (Excl. Tracks 3 & 8) 🎼 Victor Feldman - Vibes (Track 4 only)

Happy Tuesday everyone! 👋 Tonight, we are keeping it cool and stripped back.
If you need to unwind, this guitar tone is the answer.

WEEK 2 | DAY 2

[LOG 008] Grant Green - Green Street (1961)

Grant Green wasn’t your typical jazz guitarist. While others focused on complex chord voicings, Grant played single notes—crisp, biting, and linear.
He approached the guitar like a saxophone player, focusing on melody and groove above all else. By 1961, he had become Blue Note’s “secret weapon.”
In fact, you might recognize his distinct tone from Ike Quebec’s Blue & Sentimental, which we spun last week.
But Green Street is different.
It puts him front and center in a rare trio setting, stripping away everything but the essentials. It is raw, exposed, and undeniably cool.

📝 Log Note:

The magic of this session is that there is no piano. It’s just Grant, Ben Tucker on bass, and Dave Bailey on drums. Without a pianist to provide the harmony, Grant has to carry the entire weight of the music. My standout track is the standard “Alone Together”.
Since there are no chords to hide behind, his lines become punchier and more rhythmic.
He drives the whole track with just single notes and pure attitude. It’s a masterclass in tone. 🎸✨

💿 Album: Green Street 🎸 Artist: Grant Green 📅 Year: 1961 🎼 Genre: Jazz / post-bop 🏷️ Label: Blue Note [Session: April 1, 1961] 🎸 Grant Green - Guitar 🎻 Ben Tucker - Bass 🥁 Dave Bailey - Drums

Hope you all have a fantastic Tuesday night! Enjoy the vibes and see you tomorrow. 👋

#jazz #jazzlogs #grantgreen #guitarjazz #bluenote

[LOG 009] Joe Williams - Me and the Blues (1964)

Forget the “pretty” singers.
Joe Williams is a powerhouse.
Coming from the Count Basie school of swing, Joe had a voice that could shake a room, but here he shows a deeper, more intimate side.
Released on RCA Victor in 1964, this album is a masterclass in mood.
It’s dark, it’s soulful, and it feels like a late-night jazz club.

📝 Log Note:

My personal favorite here has to be “Kansas City”. There’s something about that foot-tapping rhythm and the way Joe effortlessly rides the groove—it’s the perfect blend of sophistication and raw “shout blues.” You can’t listen to this track without nodding your head. It’s a masterclass in timing and soul. 🎙️🥃

💿 Album: Me and the Blues 🎤 Artist: Joe Williams 📅 Year: 1964 🎼 Genre: Vocal Jazz / Blues 🏷️ Label: RCA Victor

🔥🔥🔥 Tonight, we are dippin’ into the good stuff!

WEEK 2 | DAY 4

[LOG 010] Hank Mobley - Dippin’ (1965)

Critics called him the “Middleweight Champion of the Tenor Sax.”
It wasn’t an insult; it meant his tone was perfect—not as aggressive as Coltrane, not as soft as Stan Getz, but round, lyrical, and swinging right in the center.
By 1965, Hank was the true backbone of the Blue Note sound.
But the secret weapon on this session is the legendary Lee Morgan.

The chemistry between them is explosive. Morgan’s fiery, sassy trumpet provides the perfect contrast to Mobley’s smooth, laid-back flow. When these two lock in on the front line, supported by Billy Higgins on drums, you are listening to the absolute peak of the Hard Bop era.

📝 Log Note:

While the whole album swings hard, my personal favorite has to be the ballad “I See Your Face Before Me”.
It’s just breathtaking. Hank slows everything down and plays with such a warm, breathy tone that it feels like he’s singing the lyrics through his horn. It’s the perfect late-night track—intimate, soulful, and deeply emotional. A true masterpiece of mood. 🎷🍷

💿 Album: Dippin’ 🎷 Hank Mobley - Tenor Sax 🎺 Lee Morgan - Trumpet 🎹 Harold Mabern - Piano 🎻 Larry Ridley - Bass 🥁 Billy Higgins - Drums

Hope you dig this one. Tomorrow we wrap up the week with a massive Essential! 👋✨

#jazzlogs #jazz #hankmobley #bluenote #vinylcommunity

caption:
ESSENTIALFRIDAYS 🎹🎷

Some albums are great. Others change history. Tonight, we spin the most famous rhythm in Jazz.

WEEK 2 | DAY 5

[LOG 011] The Dave Brubeck Quartet - Time Out (1959)

If you only own one Jazz record, it’s probably Kind of Blue. If you own two, the second one is Time Out.
In 1959, Dave Brubeck did something crazy: he decided to make an album using “weird” rhythms that you couldn’t dance to (or so they thought). The record label hated the idea. They said it would fail. Instead, it became the first Jazz album to sell a million copies and gave us the biggest hit the genre has ever seen.

📝 Log Note:

You know this track. Everyone knows “Take Five”. It’s probably the catchiest jazz tune ever written. You don’t need to understand music theory to get why this works—it just flows. There’s something about that drum beat and the way Paul Desmond’s sax floats on top that makes you feel instantly cooler just by listening to it. It’s sophisticated, it’s iconic, and it never gets old. 🎷🕶️

💿 Time Out (1959) • 🎹 The Dave Brubeck Quartet • 🏷️ Columbia • 🎼 Cool Jazz [Dave Brubeck 🎹, Paul Desmond 🎷, Eugene Wright 🎻, Joe Morello 🥁

SUNDAY BLUES 🎸🥃

WEEK 2 | SUNDAY

[LOG 012] T-Bone Walker / Big Joe Turner / Otis Spann - Super Black Blues (1969)

We are ending the week with three giants in one room.
This isn’t just an album; it’s a conversation between legends.
You have the sophisticated guitar of T-Bone Walker, the powerful voice of Big Joe Turner, and the deep piano of Otis Spann.
It’s raw, it’s loose, and it feels like you are sitting right there in the studio with them while they trade stories.

📝 Log Note:

The tracklist is short—just 4 songs—but that is all they needed. Each track is a long, unhurried jam that perfectly represents the genre, giving these masters the space to stretch out and do what they do best.

My personal favorite has to be “Here I Am Broken Hearted”. There’s something about the vibe of this track that just hits different on a Sunday evening. Big Joe Turner’s vocals are so full of soul, and the way the band plays behind him is pure emotion.
It’s slow, heavy, and exactly what I needed to hear today. 🕯️🏚️

[LOG 013] Cannonball Adderley - Know What I Mean? (1961)

By 1961, Cannonball Adderley was a soul-jazz giant.
But for this record, he wanted to explore a different texture.
He called his old friend Bill Evans (from the Kind of Blue sessions), and the result is magic.
It’s softer, deeper, and incredibly lyrical compared to his usual energetic bop.

They balance each other perfectly—warm yet cool, energetic yet relaxing. It’s the ideal sonic combination to ease into the routine.

WEEK 3 | DAY 2

[LOG 014] Nat Adderley - Naturally! (1961)

Yesterday we enjoyed the fire of Cannonball, but today I want to share something a bit more intimate.
It’s time for the younger brother, Nat Adderley.
He didn’t play the trumpet; he played the cornet. It’s a beautiful instrument with a sound that is rounder, “thicker,” and sweeter than a trumpet. It feels like a warm hug. This album is a masterclass in Soul Jazz, and look at the lineup below—he is backed by two of the greatest rhythm sections in history on the same record.

📝 Log Note:

My personal favorite is “Images”. The title fits perfectly because the music truly paints a picture. It’s a moody, hypnotic track that floats rather than swings. Nat’s tone here is incredibly open and vulnerable, creating a cinematic atmosphere that feels like a late-night walk through a foggy city. It’s pure poetry without words. 🌃🎞️

Hello everyone! Hope you are having a beautiful week so far. Today we are slowing down the pace to share something truly special. ☕️🍂

WEEK 3 | DAY 3

[LOG 015] Ella Fitzgerald & Louis Armstrong - Ella and Louis (1956)

When this album was recorded, both artists were already absolute royalty.
Ella Fitzgerald was the “First Lady of Song,” a vocal powerhouse who had conquered the swing era with her flawless pitch.
Louis Armstrong was the founding father of jazz itself, the man who practically invented jazz phrasing and the art of the solo.
Producer Norman Granz had a brilliant idea: instead of a massive big band, he backed them with the brilliant Oscar Peterson Quartet.
The result is pure magic. The contrast between Ella’s silky, elegant voice and Louis’s deep, gravelly warmth is one of the most beautiful sounds ever captured on tape.

📝 Log Note:

My personal favorite has to be “Can’t We Be Friends”.
As the opening track of the album, it perfectly sets the mood.
It’s originally a song about unrequited love, but they turn it into a playful, affectionate conversation. The exact moment when Louis’s gritty, rough voice comes in right after Ella’s incredibly smooth delivery gives me goosebumps every time.
It feels incredibly intimate, like you are sitting right there in the studio with two old friends. 🎙️🎺

💿 Album: Ella and Louis 🎤 Artists: Ella Fitzgerald & Louis Armstrong 📅 Year: 1956 🎼 Genre: Vocal Jazz / Swing 🏷️ Label: Verve 🎹 Oscar Peterson - Piano 🎸 Herb Ellis - Guitar 🎻 Ray Brown - Bass 🥁 Buddy Rich - Drums

Wishing you all a wonderful evening. Put this record on, pour yourself a drink, and let it bring a smile to your face. See you tomorrow! 🥂✨

WEEK 3 | DAY 4

[LOG 016] Sonny Clark - Blues in the Night (Recorded 1958)

If you want to know what pure, unadulterated Hard Bop piano sounds like, you listen to Sonny Clark. He was the quintessential Blue Note piano player during the late 50s.
While his life was tragically cut short, the music he left behind is immortal.
This specific trio session is a hidden gem. It was recorded in 1958 but sat in the vaults for over two decades before finally being released in 1979. It’s intimate, deeply bluesy, and swings with an effortless elegance that only Sonny possessed.

📝 Log Note:

My personal favorite has to be “Gee Baby, Ain’t I Good to You”.
Sonny’s take on this classic standard is an absolute masterclass in pacing and emotion. He doesn’t rush a single note; instead, he lets the melody breathe, giving it a smoky, relaxed, and deeply bluesy feel.
Paired with Paul Chambers’ thick bass lines, it creates a late-night atmosphere that wraps around you like a warm coat. It’s elegant, soulful, and completely irresistible. ✨

💿 Album: Blues in the Night 🎹 Artist: Sonny Clark 📅 Year: Recorded 1958 (Released 1979) 🎼 Genre: Hard Bop / Piano Jazz

Hello everyone!🎷
We finally made it to Friday.
I hope you all had a wonderful week.
To celebrate the start of the weekend, I wanted to share something truly special.
Grab a comfortable seat, lower the needle, and let the music do the rest. ☕️🍂

WEEK 3 | DAY 5

[LOG 017] Miles Davis - Kind of Blue (1959)

There are jazz albums, and then there is Kind of Blue. It’s not just a record; it’s a monument. The undisputed heavyweight champion of the jazz world. If you only own one jazz record in your entire life, it has to be this one.

In 1959, Miles Davis brought together the ultimate dream team—Coltrane, Adderley, Evans, Chambers, and Cobb—and completely threw out the rulebook. They ditched complex chord changes for “modal jazz,” giving these geniuses absolute freedom to improvise. The craziest part? Almost everything you hear on this masterpiece is a first take. No overthinking, just pure, raw genius captured on tape.

📝 Log Note:

The opener, “So What”, is the ultimate anthem.
That iconic, walking bassline by Paul Chambers (who we heard yesterday) followed by the crisp, perfectly synchronized response from the horns... it’s instantly recognizable. Then comes Miles’s solo, a masterclass in using space and silence. It’s cool, sophisticated, and absolutely timeless. 🌃✨

💿 Album: Kind of Blue 🎺 Artist: Miles Davis 📅 Year: 1959 🎼 Genre: Modal Jazz🏷️ Label: Columbia
🎺 Miles Davis - Trumpet 🎷 John Coltrane - Tenor Sax 🎷 Cannonball Adderley - Alto Sax

Wishing you all a beautiful and relaxing weekend. 🥂✨

#jazzlogs #milesdavis #kindofblue #jazz #vinylcommunity

Hello everyone and welcome to Week 4!🎹🎷
I hope you all had a fantastic and restful weekend. To kick off the new week with the right energy, we are bringing out an absolute heavyweight of the keys. ☕️🎹

WEEK 4 | DAY 1

[LOG 019] Phineas Newborn Jr. - The Great Jazz Piano of Phineas Newborn Jr. (1963)

The title of this album says it all. If we are talking about pure, unadulterated piano mastery, Phineas Newborn Jr. is in a league of his own. Often compared to giants like Art Tatum and Oscar Peterson, his technical ability on this record is simply mind-blowing.

But what makes Phineas so special isn’t just his lightning-fast speed or his signature two-handed unison runs; it’s his undeniable sense of swing. He had this incredible gift of playing the most complex, intricate lines while keeping a deep, soulful groove that never feels cold or overly academic. It’s virtuosic, but always rooted in the blues. A perfect, high-energy spin to wake up the turntable for a new week.

📝 Log Note:

My absolute favorite track on this record has to be his take on “Prelude to a Kiss”.
Ellington’s classic is already a masterpiece, but the way Phineas approaches it is pure magic. He keeps the deep, emotional core of the ballad but completely elevates it with his intricate, delicate runs. It proves that underneath all those technical fireworks, he had a deeply romantic and soulful touch. Just beautiful.

Picture this: It’s 1956, and a young Kenny Burrell just arrived in NYC from Detroit. Alfred Lion at Blue Note Records heard his warm, blues-drenched style and immediately gave him his debut as a leader.

Backed by heavyweights like his hometown friend Tommy Flanagan and bassist Paul Chambers, Burrell plays with a maturity way beyond his years. He never overplays—he just lets his elegant, buttery tone do the talking.

This album is a tight, perfectly paced journey across just 7 tracks. It is a masterclass in how to balance upbeat swing with deep, bluesy ballads without ever losing that late-night atmosphere. My absolute favorite moment has to be “Weaver of Dreams”.
Burrell’s phrasing here is so laid-back, warm, and hypnotic. He strips away any unnecessary notes, leaving only pure emotion. You drop the needle, and it just transports you.

WEEK 4 | DAY 3

[LOG 021] John Coltrane and Johnny Hartman - John Coltrane and Johnny Hartman (1963)

Picture this: It is 1963, and John Coltrane is already known for pushing the intense, fiery boundaries of jazz. But for this specific session, he decided to completely shift gears and record a dedicated ballad album. He personally sought out Johnny Hartman, a vocalist with a deep, rich baritone voice who was incredibly respected but not a massive mainstream star.
Backed by Coltrane’s legendary classic quartet, the chemistry in the studio was instantaneous. They famously recorded almost every single song in just one take. It is the ultimate, soulful meeting between a master instrumentalist and a master vocalist, creating an atmosphere that is impossible to replicate.

📝 Log Note:

This album is a brief but flawless journey, clocking in at just over 30 minutes with only 6 tracks. There is no filler here; it is pure, concentrated emotion.
My absolute favorite moment is “You Are Too Beautiful”.
Hartman’s voice wraps around you like a warm blanket, and Coltrane plays with breathtaking tenderness.
But the true hidden gem on this track is McCoy Tyner’s piano solo.
He steps in with such elegance and restraint, delivering perfectly crafted, sparkling lines that elevate the entire emotional weight of the song. Pure magic.

WEEK 4 | DAY 4

[LOG 022] Jimmy Forrest - Sit Down and Relax with Jimmy Forrest (1961)

Jimmy Forrest is often remembered for his massive R&B hit “Night Train” and his incredibly muscular, tough-tenor sound. But on this specific session for Prestige Records, he decided to show a completely different side of his musicality. He strips away the aggression and delivers an absolute masterclass in restraint, warmth, and soulful phrasing.

A fantastic detail about this session is the presence of Calvin Newborn on guitar—the brother of Phineas Newborn Jr., who we spun earlier this week! The interplay between Forrest’s breathy saxophone and Newborn’s elegant guitar lines creates a beautifully thick, smoky atmosphere that instantly transports you to a late-night jazz club.

📝 Log Note:

This album does exactly what it promises. It is unhurried, deeply rooted in the blues, and perfectly paced. My absolute favorite moment here is “The Moon Was Yellow”. Forrest’s tone on this track is so rich, breathy, and buttery that it feels like he is singing through the horn. Every note has weight and meaning, perfectly anchored by the rhythm section. You drop the needle, pour a drink, and the stress of the day just melts away.

Chet doesn’t feel the need to show off; he just delivers these beautifully lyrical, haunting lines on his trumpet that feel like they are floating in the room. This session is absolute perfection, but my favorite track has to be “September Song”.

[LOG 024] Jimmy Smith - Midnight Special (1961)

Jimmy Smith basically rewrote the rulebook for the organ in jazz. Before him, the Hammond B-3 was mostly a church instrument, but he turned it into a powerhouse of soul, blues, and hard bop. Recorded for Blue Note Records, Midnight Special captures exactly what its title promises: a deep, unhurried, late-night blues party.

What makes this session an absolute must-have is the lineup. We have the incredibly soulful Stanley Turrentine on tenor sax, and bringing it all together, Kenny Burrell on guitar. The way Burrell’s elegant, bluesy lines weave through Smith’s heavy, walking basslines on the organ pedals is just pure magic. It’s gritty, it’s elegant, and it swings effortlessly.

📝 Log Note:

The whole album is a masterpiece of groove, but the title track, “Midnight Special”, is where the magic really peaks for me. It is a slow, simmering blues that builds tension perfectly. You can practically see the smoke in the room and hear the ice clinking in the glasses. An essential Sunday night spin.

Art and I wish you a great start to the week, and welcome to Week 5!🙌🏻

This week we will be exploring new artists and great records. Today we are kicking things off with the one and only Art Pepper and one of his most iconic albums, accompanied by giants like Paul Chambers, among others. I hope you enjoy it! 🎷

WEEK 5 | DAY 1

[LOG 025] Art Pepper - Meets the Rhythm Section (1957)

At the time of this recording, Art Pepper was going through a notoriously difficult period in his life.
The legend goes that he hadn’t touched his saxophone in months and only found out about the session the very morning he was supposed to record.
To make matters even more intimidating, he walked into the studio to find Miles Davis’ legendary, tight-knit rhythm section waiting for him. Despite his horn needing repairs and his lack of practice, Pepper tapped into pure, raw emotion and delivered a completely timeless, one-take performance.

📝 Log Note:

The whole album swings incredibly hard, but my absolute favorite track has to be “Imagination”.
Red Garland sets up this beautifully delicate piano intro, and when Pepper comes in, his tone is so incredibly lyrical and vulnerable. Knowing the rough shape he was in that morning makes this gorgeous, fragile ballad even more moving. It is a true masterpiece of feeling over technique.

💿 Album: Meets the Rhythm Section

🏷️ Label: Contemporary Records

caption:

We are keeping the energy high for Week 5.
After an amazing response to yesterday’s Art Pepper story, tonight we are shifting gears to the undisputed king of the six strings. 🎸🔥

WEEK 5 | DAY 2

[LOG 026] Wes Montgomery - The Incredible Jazz Guitar of Wes Montgomery (1960)

If you have ever wondered why Wes Montgomery sounds so different from any other jazz guitarist, the secret is literally in his hands. Instead of using a pick, Wes famously played using only his thumb. The legend goes that he developed this technique while practicing late at night, trying to keep the volume down so he wouldn’t wake his wife.
The result? A completely unique, incredibly warm, and thick tone that changed jazz guitar forever. Released on Riverside Records, this album is exactly what the title promises: incredible. From blazing fast hard bop tempos to beautiful, soulful ballads, Wes makes it all sound completely effortless.

📝 Log Note:

Every single solo on this record is a masterclass, but my absolute favorite track has to be “Mister Walker”. It brings this infectious, Latin-tinged calypso groove that completely changes the pace of the album. The way Wes builds his solo here—starting with single notes, moving into those signature octaves, and finishing with full block chords—is just mind-blowing. It is impossible not to tap your foot to this one.

[LOG 027] Billie Holiday - Songs for Distingué Lovers (1957)

When it comes to pure jazz singing, there is simply no one like “Lady Day.” By 1957, Billie Holiday’s voice had changed—it was more fragile, more lived-in, but emotionally deeper than ever before.
What makes Songs for Distingué Lovers so incredibly special is the band behind her. She is surrounded by absolute giants like Ben Webster on tenor sax and Harry “Sweets” Edison on trumpet.

They don’t just back her up; they converse with her. Billie phrases her vocals exactly like a horn player, stretching the notes and singing beautifully behind the beat. It is a true jazz masterclass, not just a standard vocal pop record. It is raw, intimate, and the absolute perfect soundtrack for a quiet night.

📝 Log Note:

Every track here is a lesson in emotion and phrasing, but my absolute favorite has to be “A Foggy Day”.
The way she plays with the timing is mesmerizing, and the solos from Webster and Edison are pure elegance. A beautiful, melancholic way to end the day.

WEEK 5 | DAY 4

[LOG 028] Dexter Gordon - Go! (1962)

Often cited by Dexter himself as his favorite recording, Go! is the quintessential Blue Note session. Recorded in 1962 just before his move to Europe, it captures “Long Tall Dexter” at the absolute peak of his powers. His sound here is massive, relaxed, and filled with that signature “behind-the-beat” phrasing that makes every note feel incredibly soulful.

Accompanied by a top-tier rhythm section featuring Sonny Clark on piano, Butch Warren on bass, and Billy Higgins on drums, the chemistry is undeniable. It’s a record that defines the hard bop era: elegant, powerful, and timeless.

📝 Log Note:

While the whole album is a masterclass in swing, my absolute favorite track has to be “Cheese Cake”. The opening riff is iconic, and the way Dexter builds his solo with such confidence and wit is just mesmerizing. It’s impossible not to tap your foot to this one.

📅 Year: 1962

caption:
Good evening! 🥃🎶

Happy start to the weekend! I hope you have a great one ahead. Sit back, relax, and enjoy this incredible album by my absolute favorite pianist, Bill Evans.

WEEK 5 | DAY 5

[LOG 029] Bill Evans Trio - Portrait in Jazz (1960)

This is the album that changed the game for the piano trio. Before this, the bass and drums usually just kept time in the background.
But here, Bill Evans, Scott LaFaro, and Paul Motian play as equals. They don’t just accompany each other; they converse.
It is an incredibly sophisticated and delicate recording. At this hour of the night, Evans’ touch on the keys feels even more intimate.
It’s the perfect soundtrack to finally unwind and let the stress of the week fade away.

📝 Log Note:

The interplay on this record is legendary, but my absolute favorite has to be “Blue in Green”. It is pure, haunting melancholy and absolute perfection for this late hour. The way Evans delicately voices those chords while LaFaro weaves around them is breathtaking.
It’s pure poetry in motion.

Happy Sunday!🙌🏻🎹

WEEK 5 | SUNDAY

[LOG 030] Stan Getz & João Gilberto - Getz/Gilberto (1964)

There is no record that captures the essence of Bossa Nova quite like this one. It is the perfect marriage between cool jazz and the rhythmic heartbeat of Brazil. From the very first note of João Gilberto’s guitar, you are transported to a place of absolute calm and sophistication.

This album didn’t just introduce Bossa Nova to the world; it set a standard for elegance that has never been matched. Stan Getz’s tenor sax is pure velvet, and the addition of Astrud Gilberto’s ethereal vocals on “The Girl from Ipanema” turned this session into a global masterpiece. It is the ultimate late-night spin to soothe the soul.

📝 Log Note:

Every track is a lesson in subtlety, but my absolute favorite has to be “The Girl from Ipanema”. It’s the song that started it all, and hearing Antonio Carlos Jobim’s delicate piano work alongside João’s Portuguese vocals is pure magic. It never gets old.

Welcome to a brand new week of jazzlogs! I hope your Monday treated you well and you’re ready to settle in. There is nothing quite like dropping the needle on a great record to hit the reset button after a long day and start the week off right.

WEEK 6 | DAY 1

[LOG 031] Herbie Hancock - Takin’ Off (1962)

It is hard to believe this was Hancock’s first album as a leader.
He was only 22, yet he composed every single track and played with the maturity of a seasoned veteran.
While Bill Evans will always hold the top spot as my absolute favorite pianist, the soulful, hard bop energy Herbie brings to the keys here is just undeniable.
The horn section is legendary, featuring Freddie Hubbard and Dexter Gordon blowing pure fire over Hancock’s deep, bluesy grooves. It’s an album that perfectly balances approachability with complex, top-tier musicianship. An essential spin to get this week moving in the right direction.

📝 Log Note:

You can’t talk about this record without mentioning “Watermelon Man”. It became an instant jazz standard and a massive hit for a reason. That infectious, funky piano riff locks you in from the very first second.

caption:
Finally winding down for the day. 🎶🎷

Tuesday felt a bit long, but there is nothing quite like that moment when you finally log off, flip through the crates, and let the turntable take over.

Tonight’s spin is all about the joy of physical media, especially when you pull out a gem that streaming platforms usually miss.

WEEK 6 | DAY 2

[LOG 032] Jimmy Heath - The Quota (1961)

We are switching gears with an absolute hard bop masterpiece. Jimmy Heath had this incredible gift for making a small group sound completely massive.

The arrangements here are rich, and bringing in Julius Watkins on the French horn gives the whole session a uniquely warm texture that you rarely hear in this genre.
It creates a brilliant contrast to the sharp trumpet of Freddie Hubbard and Heath’s own smooth tenor sax.

And holding it all together, Cedar Walton provides that elegant, perfect touch on the keys that I always gravitate towards in a rhythm section. It’s sophisticated, engaging, and deeply soulful.

📝 Log Note:

The title track, “The Quota”, is exactly what you want from a hard bop tune. The melody is incredibly catchy and perfectly showcases Jimmy’s genius as a composer. Hearing the Heath brothers (Percy on bass and Tootie on drums) lock into that groove together is just pure magic.

caption:
Midweek is officially here. 🎶🎹

Wednesday nights always ask for something specific to cut through the noise of the week.
There is a different kind of magic when you put on a vocal record that doesn’t just feature a singer in front of a band, but rather a voice that breathes and moves like another instrument in the room.
Welcome to Vocal Wednesday.

WEEK 6 | DAY 3

[LOG 033] Shirley Horn - Embers and Ashes (1960)

If you are looking for late-night intimacy, this is the blueprint. Shirley Horn had this unparalleled ability to stretch time. She never rushed a lyric or a chord. What makes this album an absolute masterpiece is that she accompanies herself on the piano, allowing her to control the exact emotional weight of every single pause and phrasing.

With only eight tracks, the album doesn’t hurry to make its point. It is incredibly spacious. The way her delicate, smoky vocals blend with the elegant voicings on the keys creates a profound, melancholic atmosphere that wraps around the entire room. It is pure, unadulterated jazz.

📝 Log Note:

Listen closely to her rendition of “Like Someone in Love”.
The restraint she shows, letting the piano chords ring out into the silence before delivering the next line, is hypnotic. It is the perfect soundtrack for turning off the screens and just listening.

caption:
The weekend is almost in sight. 🎶🎺

Thursdays always bring a special kind of energy. The heaviest part of the week is behind us, and it feels like the perfect time to turn up the volume just a bit and let some brilliant hard bop fill the room.

WEEK 6 | DAY 5

[LOG 034] Freddie Hubbard - Ready for Freddie (1962)

This album is a masterclass in modern jazz. Freddie Hubbard was already established as a powerhouse on the trumpet, but here he truly steps into his own as a leader and a visionary composer. What makes this session so incredibly unique is the instrumentation. Bringing in an euphonium alongside Wayne Shorter’s tenor saxophone creates this wonderfully thick, rich texture that sets it apart from typical Blue Note releases of the era.

The rhythm section is flawless. McCoy Tyner’s piano work grounds the entire session with his signature powerful chords, while Elvin Jones drives the tempo forward with endless creativity. It’s an energetic, sophisticated record that hits the perfect balance between tight, complex arrangements and free-flowing, fiery solos.

📝 Log Note:

You have to hear “Birdlike”. It is Hubbard’s fast-paced tribute to Charlie Parker, and it’s an absolute sprint. The energy the band brings to this track is unbelievable, and Freddie’s solo is one of the most blistering, articulate pieces of trumpet playing you will ever experience.

caption:
Welcome to another essentialsFridays! 🎶🎷

I hope you all finished the week on a high note and are ready to settle into the weekend. There is no better way to leave the busy days behind and completely disconnect than by dropping the needle on a true masterpiece.

WEEK 6 | DAY 5

[LOG 035] Duke Ellington & John Coltrane (1963)

This meeting is a fundamental piece of music history. Bringing together the architect of classic jazz with the most spiritual and modern force of the saxophone might have seemed like an experiment, but the level of mutual respect in this recording transcends the speakers.

Ellington masterfully adapts to the intimate quartet format, building the perfect rhythmic and harmonic foundation for Coltrane to explore a much more restrained, melancholic, and serene sound than usual.
It is a subtle and deeply emotional record. Every single note has weight and purpose. There is no urgency, just two masters having a conversation through their instruments, creating the ultimate atmosphere to unwind.

📝 Log Note:

Anyone who knows me knows that Stevie will always hold the number one spot as my absolute favorite on the keys. But I have to admit, Duke Ellington’s touch on this particular record is just mesmerizing. The way he builds the foundation on tracks like “In a Sentimental Mood” with such delicate, rhythmic precision is pure magic.

[LOG 036] Duke Ellington & Johnny Hodges - Back to Back (1959)

We are making a comeback with two absolute giants in one room.
This isn’t just an album; it’s a late-night conversation between legends.
You have the incredibly smooth, lyrical alto saxophone of Johnny Hodges and the triumphant, relaxed piano of Duke Ellington.
It’s elegant, it’s loose, and it feels like you are sitting right there in the studio with them while they strip the blues down to its raw, emotional core.

📝 Log Note:

The tracklist is short—just 7 songs—but that is all they needed. Each track is an unhurried masterclass that perfectly represents the genre, giving these masters the space to stretch out and do what they do best.

My personal favorite has to be “Wabash Blues”. There’s something about the vibe of this track that just hits different on a Sunday evening. Johnny Hodges glides into the melody with pure perfection, and the way Ellington comps behind him with such rhythmic precision is pure emotion.
It’s slow, sophisticated, and exactly what I needed to hear today to reset. 🕯️🎷

caption:
Welcome to Week 7! 👋

We are kicking things off with absolute elegance: the deep, grounded groove of Horace Parlan.
There is no better way to start the week than with a perfectly structured hard bop session.

WEEK 7 | DAY 1

[LOG 037] Horace Parlan - Speakin’ My Piece (1960)

After childhood polio partially paralyzed his right hand, Horace Parlan developed a highly rhythmic, heavy left-hand technique that became his signature bluesy sound.
In 1960, he brought that unique groove to Blue Note Records, teaming up with the Turrentine brothers for a flawless blend of hard bop and soul jazz. Wrapped in a minimalist, dark cover, the band operates like a perfect machine where every piece fits effortlessly.

It’s an elegant, highly structured record to get you through the week.

📝 Log Note:

The texture here is incredibly solid because of how the horns interact with Parlan’s distinct chords.
My standout track is “Oh So Blue”. It’s a gorgeous, melancholy ballad that slows things down, and that’s exactly why I love it.

This is where you can truly appreciate the emotion in the room. Parlan’s delicate touch shines, but when Stanley Turrentine comes in on the tenor sax with that breathy, soulful tone, it just elevates the whole piece.

It is a total masterclass in phrasing and feeling. 🎹✨

Today is officially Guitar Tuesday, and we are keeping the elegant, late-night energy alive.
There is no better way to set the evening mood than with the flawless technique and deep swing of Joe Pass.

WEEK 7 | DAY 2

[LOG 038] Joe Pass - For Django (1964)

This isn’t just a session; it’s a master paying tribute to a legend. In 1964, Joe Pass recorded this homage to Django Reinhardt, but instead of just imitating the gypsy jazz style, he filtered those classic melodies through his own highly structured, sophisticated hard bop lens. Visually, the cover art perfectly captures the midnight aesthetic we love—a shadowy, dark room, a single guitar resting on a chair, and clean typography.

Musically, it is accessible, completely unhurried, and flows like water. It is the ultimate late-night soundtrack to wrap up your day.

📝 Log Note:

The texture here is incredibly warm because of the interplay between Pass on lead and John Pisano on rhythm guitar.
My standout track is “Django’s Castle”. It has this beautiful, laid-back swing that completely sets the mood, and that’s exactly why I love it.

This is where you can truly appreciate Pass’s flawless technique and tone. He navigates the melody with such elegance and ease without ever overplaying. Supported by a rock-solid rhythm section, it is a total masterclass in phrasing and respect for the melody. 🎸✨

caption:
VocalWednesdays!🎙️Let’s introduce the great Frank Sinatra.

Welcome to Day 3! 👋
We are diving into the grit and glamour of the most iconic voice in history.
There is no better way to power through the mid-week than with his resilient, soulful swagger.

WEEK 7 | DAY 3

[LOG 039] Frank Sinatra - That’s Life (1966)

This isn’t just a vocal album; it’s a statement of survival. By 1966, the music landscape was shifting rapidly with rock and soul taking over, and Sinatra refused to be left behind.
When he heard the song “That’s Life” on the radio, he immediately knew it was his anthem. He brought in arranger Ernie Freeman, added a heavy, blues-driven Hammond organ, and released a defiant classic that proved he was still the undisputed boss. Visually, the cover reflects that raw, mature energy—deep shadows, high contrast, and unmistakable elegance.

📝 Log Note:

The texture here is surprisingly heavy and bluesy. While the title track is the undeniable giant of the record, the rest of the album is absolutely fantastic and completely holds its own.
My standout track is still “That’s Life”. It builds with a gospel-like intensity, and that’s exactly why I love it. But listening to how he tackles the other tracks proves his flawless phrasing never missed a beat.
This is where you can truly feel Sinatra’s maturity. He isn’t just singing; he’s testifying. When the backing vocals and the brass kick in during the finale, it is a total masterclass in character. 🎙️✨
